title: Copilot SDK — Have It Your Way

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome to the Copilot SDK enterprise lab. You will scaffold a production-grade Copilot-powered application, wire up streaming and tool integration, configure Bring Your Own Model, and deploy to Azure Container Apps.

This lab assumes you are comfortable with TypeScript, Express, and Azure fundamentals. Follow the steps in this panel as you advance through the slides.

steps.title: Scaffold the SDK Project
steps.instruction:
Open a terminal and scaffold a new Copilot SDK project using the Azure Developer CLI template:

azd init --template azure-samples/copilot-sdk-service

When prompted, choose a name for your project (e.g., my-copilot-app). Accept the default settings for the Azure region and subscription.

steps.title: Install Dependencies
steps.instruction:
Navigate into your new project directory and install the dependencies:

cd my-copilot-app
npm install

Verify that @github/copilot-sdk appears in your package.json dependencies. This is the core package you will use for every integration point in this lab.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Explore the Project Structure
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and review the scaffolded project. Locate these key areas:

• src/api/ - Express server and SDK integration
• src/web/ - React-based front end
• infra/ - Bicep infrastructure definitions
• azure.yaml - azd deployment manifest

Open the main API entry point and identify where the CopilotClient is imported and configured.

steps.title: Discover Project Tasks
steps.instruction:
Open the Command Palette and type Tasks: Run Task to see what build and run tasks are available in the scaffolded project. This shows npm scripts and other configured tasks.

Dismiss the palette when done exploring. Understanding the available tasks helps you run, build, and test the project throughout this lab.

steps.title: Identify the SDK Entry Point
steps.instruction:
Find the file in src/api/ where the SDK is initialized. Look for the CopilotClient import and the createSession() call.

Review how the template connects the SDK to the Express app. Note the authentication pattern: the token is read from environment variables, not hardcoded. This is the foundation you will build on for the rest of the lab.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Command Palette: Ctrl+Shift+P

steps.title: Create a Standalone SDK Script
steps.instruction:
Create a new file called src/test-client.ts. Write a minimal SDK script that:

• Imports CopilotClient from @github/copilot-sdk
• Creates a client instance using process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN
• Opens a session with createSession()
• Sends a prompt using sendAndWait()
• Logs the response to the console

Use the code on the slide as a reference, but write it yourself.
steps.tip: Set your GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable before running the script. Use a personal access token with the copilot scope.

steps.title: Run the Test Script
steps.instruction:
Execute your test script from the terminal:

npx tsx src/test-client.ts

You should see a model response printed to the console. If you get an authentication error, verify that your GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable is set correctly.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Build a Chat Endpoint
steps.instruction:
Open the main API router file and add a POST /chat endpoint. Your endpoint should:

• Parse a message field from the request body
• Create a new session with client.createSession()
• Call session.sendAndWait(message)
• Return the response and session ID as JSON

Follow the Express patterns already established in the template code.

steps.title: Test the Chat Endpoint
steps.instruction:
Start the development server and test your endpoint. In a terminal, run:

npm run dev

Then open a second terminal and send a test request:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/chat -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"message\": \"What is the Copilot SDK?\"}"

Verify you receive a JSON response with reply and sessionId fields.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Add a Streaming Endpoint
steps.instruction:
Create a new POST /chat/stream endpoint that uses Server-Sent Events for real-time token delivery. Your endpoint should:

• Set the Content-Type header to text/event-stream
• Call session.send(message) instead of sendAndWait()
• Iterate with for await over the stream
• Write each chunk as an SSE data: event
• Send a [DONE] event when the stream completes

steps.title: Verify Streaming Behavior
steps.instruction:
Test the streaming endpoint from the terminal:

curl -N -X POST http://localhost:3000/chat/stream -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"message\": \"Explain streaming in 3 sentences\"}"

You should see individual chunks arriving in real time, followed by the [DONE] event. Compare the perceived latency to your non-streaming /chat endpoint.
steps.tip: The -N flag disables curl's output buffering so you can see chunks as they arrive.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Define a Tool Schema
steps.instruction:
Create a tools array with at least one tool definition. Build a lookup_order tool with the following JSON Schema:

• Name: lookup_order
• Description: "Look up an order by its ID"
• Parameters: an orderId string (required)

You can add a second tool if you like, such as cancel_order or list_recent_orders. Define the tools in a separate file like src/tools.ts to keep your codebase organized.

steps.title: Register Tools with a Session
steps.instruction:
Update your /chat endpoint to pass the tools array when creating a session:

const session = await client.createSession({ tools });

The SDK will handle tool orchestration automatically. When the model decides to invoke a tool, it calls your handler and feeds the result back into the conversation.

steps.title: Test Tool Invocation
steps.instruction:
Send a request that should trigger tool use:

curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/chat -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d "{\"message\": \"What is the status of order ORD-1234?\"}"

Check your server logs to confirm the tool was invoked. If the model does not call the tool, adjust your prompt to be more direct, or verify your tool schema is correctly registered.

steps.title: Check for TypeScript Errors
steps.instruction:
Open the Problems panel to check for any TypeScript compile errors in your tool definitions. Common issues include incorrect JSON Schema types, missing required fields, or import errors.

Fix any errors before proceeding. Clean tool schemas are essential — the SDK validates them at session creation time.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• New File: Ctrl+N
• Terminal: Ctrl+`
• Problems Panel: Ctrl+Shift+M

steps.title: Implement Session Persistence
steps.instruction:
Your current /chat endpoint creates a new session for every request, which means the model has no memory of previous turns. Fix this by:

• Creating an in-memory map of session IDs to session objects
• Accepting an optional sessionId in the request body
• Reusing an existing session when a valid ID is provided
• Creating a new session only when no ID is supplied

This gives your API multi-turn conversation support.

steps.title: Test Multi-Turn Conversations
steps.instruction:
Send a sequence of messages that reference previous context. For example:

Turn 1: "What is the status of order ORD-1234?"
Turn 2 (same sessionId): "Can you cancel it?"
Turn 3 (same sessionId): "How long until the refund is processed?"

Verify that the model resolves pronouns correctly ("it" refers to ORD-1234) and retains context from earlier turns. Check your session map to confirm sessions are being stored and retrieved.

steps.title: Ask Copilot for BYOM Guidance
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask for help scaffolding the Bring Your Own Model configuration:

How do I configure the Copilot SDK CopilotClient to use an Azure OpenAI endpoint with DefaultAzureCredential? Show me the provider options and environment variables I need.

Use the response as a reference for the next step.

steps.title: Configure Azure OpenAI Provider
steps.instruction:
Create a BYOM configuration that points to an Azure OpenAI endpoint. Add a new session creation path that accepts a provider option:

• Set type to "azure"
• Set baseUrl from process.env.AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT
• Use DefaultAzureCredential to fetch a bearer token
• Set wireApi to "completions"
• Specify the apiVersion

Wrap this in an environment check so your app falls back to GitHub Models when Azure credentials are not available.
steps.tip: DefaultAzureCredential from @azure/identity handles managed identity in production and developer credentials locally.

steps.title: Set Environment Variables in Settings
steps.instruction:
Open Settings and search for terminal.integrated.env to configure environment variables for your integrated terminal. Add AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT and GITHUB_TOKEN so they are available every time you open a terminal in this workspace.

Alternatively, create a .env file in your project root. The key point is that secrets live in configuration, never in source code.

steps.title: Verify BYOM Fallback
steps.instruction:
Test both paths. First, ensure the app works with GitHub Models when no Azure environment variables are set. Then set AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT and verify the provider configuration is picked up.

If you do not have an Azure OpenAI deployment available, confirm the fallback logic works correctly by logging which provider is active at startup. The key outcome is that your code cleanly supports both backends.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Settings: Ctrl+,
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Review Infrastructure as Code
steps.instruction:
Open the infra/ directory and review the Bicep files. Identify the resources being provisioned:

• Azure Container Apps Environment
• Container App (your service)
• Container Registry
• Application Insights
• Log Analytics Workspace

Check the azure.yaml manifest to see how the deployment pipeline maps your source code to infrastructure. Note how environment variables and secrets are configured.

steps.title: Deploy with azd
steps.instruction:
Deploy your application to Azure:

azd up

The CLI will provision infrastructure, build your application, and deploy it. This may take several minutes on the first run. When it completes, note the endpoint URL printed in the output.

Test your deployed endpoint with curl to confirm it is working in the cloud.
steps.tip: Use azd up --environment staging to deploy to a separate environment for testing before production.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the bottom panel for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Add Custom Telemetry
steps.instruction:
The template configures Application Insights automatically, but production Copilot apps need custom metrics. Add telemetry for:

• Token usage: Log prompt and completion token counts per session
• Latency: Measure time-to-first-token and total response time
• Tool calls: Track which tools are invoked and their success/failure rate

Use applicationinsights custom events or your preferred logging framework.

steps.title: Check Server Output Logs
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel and select your development server channel. Send a few test requests to your local server and watch for telemetry events being logged. Verify that token counts, latency measurements, and tool-call tracking appear in the output.

Local output verification ensures your telemetry pipeline works before deploying to Azure.

steps.title: Verify Telemetry in the Portal
steps.instruction:
Send several requests to your deployed endpoint, then open Application Insights in the Azure Portal. Navigate to Transaction search and verify that:

• Request traces appear for your /chat and /chat/stream endpoints
• Custom events include token usage metrics
• Dependency calls to the Copilot service are tracked

If custom events are missing, check that your telemetry client is flushing before the process exits.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Secure Token Storage
steps.instruction:
Move your GitHub token from environment variables to Azure Key Vault. Update your application to:

• Use @azure/identity with DefaultAzureCredential
• Fetch the token from Key Vault at startup using @azure/keyvault-secrets
• Cache the token in memory to avoid repeated Key Vault calls
• Implement a refresh mechanism for token rotation

Verify that no secrets appear in your source code or deployment configuration.

steps.title: Add Rate Limiting
steps.instruction:
Implement per-user rate limiting on your /chat and /chat/stream endpoints. Your rate limiter should:

• Identify users by API key or JWT claims
• Limit requests to a configurable threshold (e.g., 60 requests per minute)
• Return HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header when the limit is exceeded
• Log rate-limit events for monitoring

Use a middleware pattern so the rate limiter applies consistently across all chat endpoints.
steps.tip: For production, use a distributed rate limiter backed by Redis. For this lab, an in-memory implementation is sufficient.

steps.title: Validate Input and Output
steps.instruction:
Add input validation and output filtering to your chat endpoints:

• Validate that incoming messages are non-empty strings under a maximum length
• Validate tool-call arguments against your JSON schemas before executing
• Filter model responses to strip any sensitive patterns (e.g., tokens, connection strings)
• Log all validation failures for security auditing

Test with malformed requests to ensure your validation returns appropriate HTTP 400 responses.

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! You have completed the Copilot SDK enterprise lab.

You built a production-grade Copilot-powered application from the ground up:

• Scaffolded a project with the azd template
• Created synchronous and streaming chat endpoints
• Integrated tool definitions with automatic orchestration
• Implemented multi-turn conversation persistence
• Configured Bring Your Own Model with Azure OpenAI fallback
• Deployed to Azure Container Apps with azd up
• Added custom telemetry for token usage and latency
• Secured the application with Key Vault, rate limiting, and input validation

Next up: MCP Maximizer to extend your SDK app with external tool servers, or Multi-Agent Multi-Repo Roundup for orchestrating agents across repositories.
